div.p: POSIX-defined functions safe to call from signal handlers
✓ Async-Signal-Safe Can be called from signal handlers without risk of deadlock or corruption
✗ NOT Async-Signal-Safe May cause deadlock, corruption, or undefined behavior in handlers

Main Thread: printf("Hello")
↓ Acquires stdout lock ↓ [SIGNAL ARRIVES] Signal Handler: printf("Signal!")
↓ Tries to acquire stdout lock ↓ BLOCKED - lock already held ↓ DEADLOCK

Key Insight: Signal handlers can interrupt your program at ANY point, including while inside a function that holds internal locks. If the handler calls the same function (or any function sharing the same locks), it will wait forever for a lock that the interrupted code already holds.
write() - Raw syscall read() - Raw syscall pipe() - Create pipe close() - Close fd
_exit() - Immediate exit fork() - Create process exec*() - Execute program waitpid() - Wait for child
signal() - Set handler sigaction() - Set handler sigprocmask() - Block signals kill() - Send signal
memcpy() - Copy memory strcpy() - Copy string strlen() - String length strerror() - Error string
printf() - Uses locks fprintf() - Uses locks scanf() - Uses locks fgets() - Uses locks perror() - Uses stdio
malloc() - Heap locks free() - Heap locks realloc() - Heap locks calloc() - Heap locks
exit() - Calls atexit strdup() - Calls malloc system() - Uses fork/wait syslog() - Internal locks
